quiz_questions.question_text: Photometry measures which property of celestial objects?

options.text: Chemical composition
Brightness (flux)
Temperature only
Distance directly

quiz_questions.explanation: Photometry is the measurement of the brightness (or flux) of celestial objects. By carefully measuring how bright a star appears, astronomers can determine distances, variability, and even detect exoplanets.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spectroscopy involves splitting light into:

options.text: Equal-sized blocks
Gravitational waves
Sound waves
A spectrum of different wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: Spectroscopy splits light into its component wavelengths (colors), revealing a detailed spectrum. Each chemical element produces unique spectral lines, making spectroscopy the primary tool for determining stellar composition.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Doppler shift of a star's spectrum can reveal its:

options.text: Age in years
Radial velocity — whether it is moving toward or away from us
Color to the naked eye
Size in kilometers

quiz_questions.explanation: When a light source moves toward us, its wavelengths are compressed (blueshift). When it moves away, wavelengths stretch (redshift). This Doppler shift directly measures the radial velocity along our line of sight.

quiz_questions.question_text: Astrometry is the precise measurement of:

options.text: Positions and motions of stars
Star temperatures
The chemical composition of nebulae
The age of the universe

quiz_questions.explanation: Astrometry involves extremely precise measurement of where stars are and how they move. It is used to determine parallax distances, detect exoplanets through stellar wobble, and map the structure of our galaxy.

quiz_questions.question_text: CCD cameras are used in modern telescopes because they:

options.text: Convert light into digital signals with very high sensitivity
Can only detect infrared light
Are cheaper than film cameras
Do not require a telescope to work

quiz_questions.explanation: CCD (charge-coupled device) cameras convert photons into electronic signals with much greater efficiency than photographic film — often detecting over 90% of incoming photons. They are the standard detector in modern astronomy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Apparent magnitude measures how bright a star:

options.text: Was when it first formed
Would appear from the Moon
Actually is at a standard distance
Appears from Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Apparent magnitude is how bright a star looks from our vantage point on Earth. It depends on both the star's actual luminosity and its distance. A closer dim star can appear brighter than a distant luminous one.

quiz_questions.question_text: Absolute magnitude measures how bright a star would be if it were at a standard distance of:

options.text: 10 parsecs
1 light-year
100 parsecs
1 AU

quiz_questions.explanation: Absolute magnitude standardizes brightness by placing all stars at the same hypothetical distance of 10 parsecs (32.6 light-years). This allows fair comparison of stars' true luminosities.

quiz_questions.question_text: Emission lines in a spectrum appear when:

options.text: Hot, low-density gas emits light at specific wavelengths
Telescopes malfunction
Stars absorb all incoming light
Light passes through a vacuum

quiz_questions.explanation: Hot, low-density gas emits photons at specific wavelengths corresponding to electron transitions in its atoms. Each element has a unique set of emission lines — like a fingerprint identifying its presence.

quiz_questions.question_text: Absorption lines in a spectrum appear when:

options.text: Stars emit extra light at certain colors
Light travels through a vacuum
The telescope lens is dirty
Cooler gas between a source and observer absorbs specific wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: When light from a hot source passes through cooler gas, the gas absorbs specific wavelengths, creating dark lines in the spectrum. The pattern of absorption lines identifies which elements are present in the intervening gas.

quiz_questions.question_text: Multi-wavelength imaging means observing the same object in:

options.text: Multiple copies of the same photograph
Multiple types of light (radio, infrared, visible, X-ray, etc.)
Multiple times on the same night
Multiple sizes of the same telescope

quiz_questions.explanation: Different wavelengths reveal different physical processes. Visible light shows stars, infrared shows dust and cool gas, X-rays show extremely hot gas, and radio shows magnetic fields. Combining them gives a complete picture.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'photometry'?

options.text: The study of how photographs are developed
The study of photosynthesis in plants
The measurement of a star's chemical composition
The measurement of the brightness or flux of celestial objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Photometry (photo = light, metry = measurement) quantifies how much light an object emits or reflects. Time-series photometry tracks brightness changes, which can reveal eclipsing binaries, variable stars, and transiting exoplanets.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'spectroscopy'?

options.text: A method of amplifying telescope images
The technique of viewing objects through colored glass
The study of sound waves from space
The technique of analyzing light by separating it into its component wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: Spectroscopy decomposes light into a spectrum revealing absorption and emission lines. From these, astronomers determine composition, temperature, density, magnetic fields, and radial velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'astrometry'?

options.text: The study of horoscopes and zodiac signs
The calculation of a star's temperature
The precise measurement of star positions and motions in the sky
The measurement of asteroid sizes

quiz_questions.explanation: Astrometry is one of the oldest branches of astronomy. Today, ESA's Gaia spacecraft is performing astrometry on nearly 2 billion stars, mapping the Milky Way in unprecedented detail.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'Doppler shift'?

options.text: The change in a star's brightness over time
The change in wavelength of light (or sound) due to relative motion between source and observer
The splitting of light by a prism
The bending of light around a massive object

quiz_questions.explanation: The Doppler effect: approaching objects show blueshifted (shorter wavelength) light; receding objects show redshifted (longer wavelength) light. The amount of shift is proportional to the velocity. This is used to measure stellar motions and discover exoplanets.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'apparent magnitude'?

options.text: A measure of how bright a celestial object appears from Earth's vantage point
A measure of how bright a star truly is
The size a star appears to the naked eye
The distance from Earth to a star

quiz_questions.explanation: The apparent magnitude scale was invented by Hipparchus (~150 BCE). Lower numbers mean brighter: the Sun is -26.7, full Moon is -12.6, and the faintest naked-eye stars are about +6. Each step of 1 magnitude is a factor of ~2.5 in brightness.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'absolute magnitude'?

options.text: The maximum brightness a star can ever reach
The brightness of a star as seen from Earth
The total amount of energy a star has produced
A measure of a star's intrinsic brightness, as if placed at a standard distance of 10 parsecs

quiz_questions.explanation: Absolute magnitude removes the effect of distance, allowing fair comparison. The Sun's absolute magnitude is +4.83 (a fairly ordinary star), while Rigel's is about -7 (extremely luminous).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'CCD' (Charge-Coupled Device)?

options.text: A chemical compound used to develop photographs
A type of telescope mirror coating
A radio antenna for detecting cosmic signals
An electronic light detector that converts photons into digital signals for imaging

quiz_questions.explanation: CCDs are silicon chips that accumulate electric charge when photons strike them. They detect ~90% of incoming photons (vs. ~2% for film). Nearly every modern telescope uses CCD or similar CMOS detectors.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are 'UBVRI filters'?

options.text: Brightness categories for classifying stars from dim to bright
Standard color filters (Ultraviolet, Blue, Visual, Red, Infrared) used to measure star brightness at specific wavelengths
Chemical treatments applied to telescope mirrors
Five types of telescopes used in observatories

quiz_questions.explanation: UBVRI filters allow astronomers to measure a star's brightness at specific wavelengths. Comparing brightness through different filters reveals a star's color and temperature — a technique called photometric color indices.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'image processing' in astronomy?

options.text: Polishing telescope mirrors and lenses
Printing telescope photos on paper
Drawing pictures of what astronomers see through telescopes
Using software to enhance, clean, and analyze digital telescope images

quiz_questions.explanation: Raw telescope images often need calibration (removing noise, correcting for detector imperfections), stacking (combining multiple exposures), and enhancement (adjusting contrast or color mapping) to reveal faint structures.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'redshift'?

options.text: The literal color of all distant stars
The brightening of a star at sunset
The change of a star's color to red as it ages
The stretching of light to longer wavelengths, indicating an object is moving away from the observer

quiz_questions.explanation: Redshift occurs when spectral lines shift toward longer (redder) wavelengths. Cosmological redshift is caused by the expansion of space itself and is used to measure galaxy distances and the expansion rate of the universe.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomer observes a star's spectrum and sees hydrogen absorption lines shifted toward the red end. What can she conclude?

options.text: The star is unusually hot
The star is running out of hydrogen fuel
The telescope has a red filter on
The star is moving away from us — its light is redshifted due to the Doppler effect

quiz_questions.explanation: Redshifted absorption lines directly indicate recession velocity. The amount of shift reveals the speed. This technique is used to measure stellar radial velocities, find exoplanets, and determine galaxy recession speeds.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures a star's apparent magnitude as +1 and finds its distance is 10 parsecs. What is its absolute magnitude?

options.text: Also +1, because at 10 parsecs the apparent and absolute magnitudes are equal by definition
0, because magnitude resets at 10 parsecs
+10, because you multiply magnitude by distance
-1, because absolute is always the negative of apparent

quiz_questions.explanation: By definition, absolute magnitude is the apparent magnitude a star would have if placed at exactly 10 parsecs. If a star is already at 10 parsecs, its apparent magnitude equals its absolute magnitude directly.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two stars have the same apparent magnitude (+2.0). Star A is 5 parsecs away; Star B is 50 parsecs away. Which star is actually more luminous?

options.text: Star A — closer stars are always more luminous
Neither — you need spectroscopy to determine luminosity
They are equally luminous since they appear equally bright
Star B — it appears equally bright despite being 10 times farther, so it must be intrinsically much brighter

quiz_questions.explanation: Brightness decreases with the square of distance. Star B is 10x farther, so light from it is spread over 100x the area. For it to appear equally bright at 100x the distance, it must be 100 times more luminous than Star A.

quiz_questions.question_text: A team observes a star's spectrum and identifies lines matching iron, hydrogen, and calcium. What technique did they use and what did it reveal?

options.text: Spectroscopy — the absorption or emission lines reveal the chemical composition of the star's atmosphere
Radar — they bounced signals off the star to detect its elements
Astrometry — they measured the star's precise position to determine composition
Photometry — they measured the star's brightness at different wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: Each element produces unique spectral lines at specific wavelengths. Matching observed lines to known laboratory spectra is how astronomers determine what stars (and other objects) are made of — even from billions of light-years away.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomer notices that a star's brightness dips by 1% every 3.5 days in a regular pattern. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: The telescope is malfunctioning every 3.5 days
The star is rotating and has dark spots that face us every 3.5 days
An exoplanet is transiting (passing in front of) the star, periodically blocking a small fraction of its light
A nearby star is eclipsing it every 3.5 days

quiz_questions.explanation: Periodic brightness dips are the hallmark of the transit method for detecting exoplanets. The depth of the dip reveals the planet's size relative to the star, and the period gives the orbital period. This is how Kepler and TESS discovered thousands of exoplanets.

quiz_questions.question_text: An observatory uses adaptive optics — a deformable mirror that changes shape hundreds of times per second. What problem does this solve?

options.text: It makes the telescope zoom in and out like a camera
It focuses radio waves into visible light
It corrects for atmospheric turbulence in real-time, producing sharper images from the ground
It filters out moonlight during observations

quiz_questions.explanation: Adaptive optics measures atmospheric distortion using a reference star (natural or artificial laser guide star) and rapidly adjusts the mirror to compensate. Ground-based telescopes with adaptive optics can rival the sharpness of some space telescope images.

quiz_questions.question_text: A team combines radio data from telescopes in Hawaii, Chile, and Spain to create an image. What technique are they using and why?

options.text: Spectroscopy — they are splitting radio waves into a spectrum
Interferometry — combining signals from widely separated telescopes creates an effective aperture as large as the distance between them
Photometry — they are measuring brightness from three locations
Three telescopes always produce better images than one

quiz_questions.explanation: Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) combines data from telescopes across the globe. The Event Horizon Telescope used this technique with telescopes on multiple continents to produce the first image of a black hole's shadow in 2019.

quiz_questions.question_text: A star shows a periodic blueshift and redshift in its spectral lines over several days, but no brightness change. What might be causing this?

options.text: The star is spinning faster and slower over time
An orbiting planet (or companion) is causing the star to wobble, producing alternating Doppler shifts
The star is moving in a straight line toward and away from us
The atmosphere is causing the shifts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is the radial velocity (Doppler wobble) method for detecting exoplanets. The planet's gravity tugs the star, making it wobble slightly. The period and amplitude of the Doppler shifts reveal the planet's orbital period and minimum mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: Gaia, ESA's space observatory, is measuring the positions of nearly 2 billion stars with incredible precision. What primary technique does Gaia use?

options.text: Astrometry — precisely measuring star positions and motions to create a 3D map of the Milky Way
Spectroscopy — splitting light from 2 billion stars simultaneously
Photometry — only measuring the brightness of stars
Radio astronomy — listening for signals from each star

quiz_questions.explanation: Gaia is the most ambitious astrometry mission ever. By measuring stellar positions to micro-arcsecond precision, it determines parallax distances and proper motions for billions of stars, revolutionizing our understanding of the Milky Way's structure and dynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student asks: 'Why do astronomers need so many different observational techniques? Can't they just take a picture?' What is the best response?

options.text: Different techniques are just different names for the same thing
A picture is indeed sufficient for all astronomical research
Multiple techniques are used only because astronomers want more publications
Different techniques reveal different physical properties — imaging shows structure, spectroscopy reveals composition, photometry tracks brightness, and astrometry maps positions

quiz_questions.explanation: A photograph of a star tells you where it is and roughly how bright it looks. But spectroscopy reveals what it's made of and how fast it's moving. Photometry tracks how its brightness changes over time. Astrometry maps its precise motion. Each technique answers different questions.
